title: India and Nepal Pivot Bilateral Ties Beyond Energy to AI, Startups, Renewables
lede: External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar hosted Nepal's Foreign Minister Shisir Khanal in Delhi on June 6, 2026, signalling a deliberate expansion of the bilateral relationship into emerging domains including artificial intelligence, startups, information technology, and renewable energy.

sections.context: The meeting between EAM Jaishankar and FM Khanal represents a calculated repositioning of India-Nepal bilateral engagement beyond the traditional energy-focused framework that has anchored the relationship for decades. Jaishankar explicitly acknowledged the 'very special relationship' built on people-to-people ties, cultural and religious traditions, and shared trust—foundations that have enabled steady development in established sectors including trade, commerce, investments, energy, development cooperation, education, disaster response and culture. However, the emphasis on 'many other areas' and 'newer domains' signals deliberate institutional attention to modernizing the partnership. The timing follows Khanal's previous engagement with Indian leadership approximately two months prior in Mauritius, suggesting continuity and momentum in high-level diplomatic contact. This pivot occurs against a backdrop where [[india-np-india-and-nepal-expand-bilateral-agenda-beyond-energy-i-2026|India and Nepal have already begun expanding their bilateral agenda beyond energy]] and where [[india-np-india-nepal-bilateral-pivot-from-energy-to-ai-startups-2026|bilateral ties are pivoting toward AI, startups, and renewable sectors]].
sections.threads: The expansion into AI, startups, and information technology reflects a broader strategic recognition that India's technological capabilities and Nepal's emerging startup ecosystem represent untapped bilateral potential. Jaishankar's framing of renewable energy alongside traditional hydropower cooperation suggests India may be recalibrating its approach to energy partnerships in ways that extend beyond hydropower agreements that have historically dominated the relationship. The reference to maintaining security along the 'long and uniquely open border' and ensuring 'uninterrupted fuel supplies' during the West Asia crisis indicates that traditional security cooperation and energy resilience remain foundational, even as new domains are activated. This multi-track approach aligns with earlier signalling that [[india-np-india-nepal-seek-to-decisively-shift-trajectory-of-2026|India and Nepal seek to decisively shift the trajectory of bilateral relations]] across multiple institutional frameworks, and reflects sustained [[india-np-nepal-fms-india-visit-signals-institutional-deepening-a-2026|institutional deepening across bilateral engagements]].
sections.signal: Intelligence analysts should monitor whether India and Nepal establish formal working groups or institutional mechanisms in the identified 'newer domains'—AI, startups, and renewable energy—within the next 6-12 months. The substantive translation of Jaishankar's remarks into bilateral agreements, memoranda of understanding, or capacity-building initiatives will indicate whether this represents a genuine strategic recalibration or rhetorical expansion without structural follow-through. Particular attention should be paid to whether renewable energy cooperation emerges as a counterbalance to traditional hydropower partnerships, which have historically proven contentious. The commitment to ensure 'uninterrupted fuel supplies' during West Asian instability suggests India may leverage energy security as a foundation for deeper partnerships in emerging technology sectors. Watch for announcements regarding [[india-np-india-nepal-bilateral-expansion-eam-jaishankar-signals-2026|EAM Jaishankar signalling a bilateral pivot]] in the weeks and months following this June 6 meeting, particularly any formal institutional arrangements or joint declarations that operationalize the 'newer domains' referenced in his opening remarks.
